ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a species-specific entry for Scylla serrata (Sri Lanka mud crab); closest_parent 'crab (whole or parts)' used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GR covers crabs as a category; no species-specific code for Scylla serrata exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 47492001 represents crab as a substance; no species-specific code for Scylla serrata is available.

serving.description: 1 whole crab portion (approx. 500 g whole weight, ~200 g edible meat)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Sri Lanka crab is one whole crab weighing approximately 500 g live weight, yielding roughly 200 g of edible crab meat after shell removal.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, blue, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15139); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods entry for blue crab and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for mud crab (Scylla serrata)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No USDA or HPB entry exists specifically for Scylla serrata (Sri Lanka mud crab). Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab (NDB 15139) and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for mud crab, which show closely comparable macro profiles. The adaptation is conservative and reflects the lean, high-protein, low-fat nature of cooked crab meat. Sodium is elevated to reflect typical restaurant preparation with salt and sauces.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant seafood foods — analogous estimate based on published GI data for cooked shellfish and crustaceans; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sri Lanka mud crab (Scylla serrata). Crab meat is a high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate food (carbohydrate_g ≈ 0 per 100 g). Per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), protein-dominant seafood and shellfish have a GI of approximately 5 or effectively zero due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content. GI is set to 5 (low) as the closest defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 200 g serving is 0.0 given carbohydrate_g = 0.

hypertension.guidance: Sri Lanka crab as served in restaurants typically contains 300–500 mg sodium per 100 g due to salt, sauces, and marinades; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce preparations to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sri Lanka crab contains virtually no carbohydrate (≈0 g per 100 g) and has a glycemic index of approximately 5, making it a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is not served with high-sugar sauces such as sweet chilli.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, and moderate cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sri Lanka crab is low in saturated fat (~0.3 g per 100 g) and provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids; moderate cholesterol content (~78 mg per 100 g) is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet, though individuals with severe hypercholesterolaemia should monitor total weekly shellfish intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sri Lanka Crab refers to the mud crab species Scylla serrata, imported live from Sri Lanka and widely served in Chinese seafood restaurants in Singapore. It is prized for its large size, dense and sweet crab meat, and thick roe-filled shell. In Singapore's Chinese seafood cuisine, Sri Lanka crab is most commonly prepared as chilli crab (stir-fried in a tangy tomato-chilli sauce) or black pepper crab (wok-fried with coarsely ground black pepper and butter), though it is also steamed plain to highlight its natural sweetness. The crab is typically cooked live to preserve freshness and flavour. Nutritionally, Sri Lanka crab is a lean, high-protein food providing approximately 97 kcal and 19.4 g protein per 100 g of edible meat, with very low fat (1.8 g) and negligible carbohydrate. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12, selenium, zinc, and phosphorus. The glycemic index is effectively zero (estimated GI ≈ 5), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate in plain preparations but can be elevated in restaurant dishes with sauces. Sri Lanka crab is a premium seafood item in Singapore's hawker and restaurant culture, often associated with celebratory meals and Chinese New Year dining.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Sri Lanka crab?
Is Sri Lanka crab good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Sri Lanka crab and chilli crab?
Is Sri Lanka crab high in sodium?
What species is Sri Lanka crab?
How is Sri Lanka crab cooked in Singapore?
What nutrients are in crab meat?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Sri Lanka mud crab (Scylla serrata) as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab and FAO/INFOODS SEA mud crab data, which are closely comparable species. GI is an analogous estimate from published shellfish GI data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the macro profile of cooked crab meat is well-established across multiple approved sources and the adaptation is conservative.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for cooked crab meat; crab contains no glycemic carbohydrates — confirmed zero. 2. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero as crab meat contains no free sugars — confirmed zero. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero as crab meat is an animal product with no dietary fibre — naturally zero. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data — confirmed zero. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1: not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy source used for this adaptation; value is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 6. GI is set to 5 (low) as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Scylla serrata exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 given carbohydrate_g = 0. 7. Sodium value of 395 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant preparation; plain steamed crab would have lower sodium (~250 mg). 8. The cuisine tag 'Lobster' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has not been carried forward as a food identity claim. 9. Price data from field survey (SGD 12.10 per 100 g) is noted but not included in the RESULT template as it is not a supported field.
